blocks.text: As for me, in the first year of Darius the Mede, I stood up to support and strengthen him.
10.10–11.1: Gabriel strengthens Daniel for the revelation.
11.1 : The meaning is unclear; perhaps Gabriel supported Darius the Mede, who acted favorably toward Daniel (see 5.31; 6.23–27).

“Now I will announce the truth to you. Three more kings shall arise in Persia. The fourth shall be far richer than all of them, and when he has become strong through his riches, he shall stir up all against the kingdom of Greece.
2 : There were more than four of Persia, but four signals totality. , Persian monarchs enjoyed the reputation of being fabulously wealthy.
Then a warrior king shall arise, who shall rule with great dominion and take action as he pleases.
3 : , Alexander the Great.

And while still rising in power, his kingdom shall be broken and divided toward the four winds of heaven, but not to his posterity, nor according to the dominion with which he ruled; for his kingdom shall be uprooted and go to others besides these.
11.2–4: Persian and Greek succession. , see 7.6n.; 8.3n.
4 : refers to Alexander's death in 323 and the subsequent division of his territories; see 2.41n.; 7.7–8n. , see 7.2n.
“Then the king of the south shall grow strong, but one of his officers shall grow stronger than he and shall rule a realm greater than his own realm.
5 : , Ptolemy I (305–283 ) of Egypt; his is Seleucus I Nicanor (312–281 ) who ruled Syria.

After some years they shall make an alliance, and the daughter of the king of the south shall come to the king of the north to ratify the agreement. But she shall not retain her power, and his offspring shall not endure. She shall be given up, she and her attendants and her child and the one who supported her. “In those times
6 : In 250, Ptolemy II Philadelphus (285–246 ) gave his daughter, Berenice, in marriage to the , Antiochus II Theos (261–246 ). Rivals murdered Berenice and her son.
a branch from her roots shall rise up in his place. He shall come against the army and enter the fortress of the king of the north, and he shall take action against them and prevail.
7 : , Ptolemy III Euergetes (246–221 ), Berenice's brother, who invaded Seleucid territory.

Even their gods, with their idols and with their precious vessels of silver and gold, he shall carry off to Egypt as spoils of war. For some years he shall refrain from attacking the king of the north;
then the latter shall invade the realm of the king of the south, but will return to his own land.
9 : Seleucus II Callinicus (246–225 ) invaded Ptolemy's territory in reprisal.
“His sons shall wage war and assemble a multitude of great forces, which shall advance like a flood and pass through, and again shall carry the war as far as his fortress.
10 : Seleucus II's were Seleucus III Ceraunus (225–223 ) and the militarily successful Antiochus III the Great (223–187 ).
Moved with rage, the king of the south shall go out and do battle against the king of the north, who shall muster a great multitude, which shall, however, be defeated by his enemy.
11 : Ptolemy IV Philopator (221–204 ) Antiochus III at Raphia in 217.
When the multitude has been carried off, his heart shall be exalted, and he shall overthrow tens of thousands, but he shall not prevail.
For the king of the north shall again raise a multitude, larger than the former, and after some years he shall advance with a great army and abundant supplies.
13 : Antiochus III had military successes in Syria and Palestine (212–205 ).
“In those times many shall rise against the king of the south. The lawless among your own people shall lift themselves up in order to fulfill the vision, but they shall fail.
14 : , rebellions within Egypt against the Ptolemies. , the pro-Antiochus faction in Judea.

Then the king of the north shall come and throw up siegeworks, and take a well-fortified city. And the forces of the south shall not stand, not even his picked troops, for there shall be no strength to resist.
15 : After claiming Jerusalem in the battle of Panion in 200, Antiochus III claimed victory at Sidon, the .
But he who comes against him shall take the actions he pleases, and no one shall withstand him. He shall take a position in the beautiful land, and all of it shall be in his power.
16 : , cf. 11.3,36. Antiochus III is at the height of his power. , Jerusalem (cf. 8.9; 11.41).

He shall set his mind to come with the strength of his whole kingdom, and he shall bring terms of peace and perform them. In order to destroy the kingdom, he shall give him a woman in marriage; but it shall not succeed or be to his advantage.
17 : Antiochus III offered his daughter Cleopatra to Ptolemy V Epiphanes (204–180 ). Cleopatra undermined her father's intention, which had been to ruin her husband.
Afterward he shall turn to the coastlands, and shall capture many. But a commander shall put an end to his insolence; indeed, he shall turn his insolence back upon him.
18 : , territories in Greece and Thrace, which Antiochus III tried to claim. He was defeated by the Romans at Magnesia in 189. , Scipio.
Then he shall turn back toward the fortresses of his own land, but he shall stumble and fall, and shall not be found.
11.5–19: The Seleucids and the Ptolemies.
19 : , Antiochus III died in 187 after raiding a temple in Elemais.
“Then shall arise in his place one who shall send an official for the glory of the kingdom; but within a few days he shall be broken, though not in anger or in battle.
20 : , Seleucus IV Philopator (187–175 ) who sent the Heliodorus to the Jerusalem Temple for funds (2 Macc 3).
In his place shall arise a contemptible person on whom royal majesty had not been conferred; he shall come in without warning and obtain the kingdom through intrigue.
21 : , Antiochus IV (175–164 ) usurped the throne from his nephew and had him assassinated.
Armies shall be utterly swept away and broken before him, and the prince of the covenant as well.
22 : , the High Priest Onias III; see 9.26n.
And after an alliance is made with him, he shall act deceitfully and become strong with a small party.
23 : Antiochus IV made with the king of Pergamon.

Without warning he shall come into the richest parts of the province and do what none of his predecessors had ever done, lavishing plunder, spoil, and wealth on them. He shall devise plans against strongholds, but only for a time.
24 : Antiochus IV was to an unprecedented degree with gifts. He then taxed Persia to refill his coffers.

He shall stir up his power and determination against the king of the south with a great army, and the king of the south shall wage war with a much greater and stronger army. But he shall not succeed, for plots shall be devised against him
by those who eat of the royal rations. They shall break him, his army shall be swept away, and many shall fall slain.
25–26 : Antiochus IV is blamed for initiating war with Egypt (see also 1 Macc 1.16). , Ptolemy's advisors, Eulaeus and Lenaeus, whose advice was catastrophic.
The two kings, their minds bent on evil, shall sit at one table and exchange lies. But it shall not succeed, for there remains an end at the time appointed.
27 : , Antiochus IV and Ptolemy VI Philometor (180–145 ).
He shall return to his land with great wealth, but his heart shall be set against the holy covenant. He shall work his will, and return to his own land.
11.21–28: Antiochus IV Epiphanes.
28 : , Antiochus's attack on Jerusalem and the Temple in 169 (1 Macc 1.20–21; 2 Macc 5.11–21; see also 9.26n.).
“At the time appointed he shall return and come into the south, but this time it shall not be as it was before.
29 : introduces the climactic events of history.

For ships of Kittim shall come against him, and he shall lose heart and withdraw. He shall be enraged and take action against the holy covenant. He shall turn back and pay heed to those who forsake the holy covenant.
30 : , refers generally to places in the eastern Mediterranean (Gen 10.4; 1 Macc 1.1). Here it is Rome, which stopped Antiochus when he invaded Egypt in 168.
Forces sent by him shall occupy and profane the temple and fortress. They shall abolish the regular burnt offering and set up the abomination that makes desolate.
30–31 : , Antiochus sent troops to Jerusalem in 167 (1 Macc 1.29–32; 2 Macc 5.24–26). , an altar erected to Zeus Olympios in the Temple (8.10–12n.; 1 Macc 1.54; see also Mk 13.14).
He shall seduce with intrigue those who violate the covenant; but the people who are loyal to their God shall stand firm and take action.
32 : , the High Priest Menelaus and others who supported extreme Hellenization in Jerusalem. , factions who opposed the Hellenizing party.
The wise among the people shall give understanding to many; for some days, however, they shall fall by sword and flame, and suffer captivity and plunder.
When they fall victim, they shall receive a little help, and many shall join them insincerely.
34 : , the wise lack strong support.
Some of the wise shall fall, so that they may be refined, purified, and cleansed, until the time of the end, for there is still an interval until the time appointed.
33–35 : (Heb “maskilim”) who instruct others about God's hidden plans (cf. Isa 53.11–12).
35 : , some will be martyred (cf. 1 Macc 1.63; 2 Macc 7). , death will purify the wise (Ps 17.3; Jer 6.29; 9.7; Wis 3.1–8; Sir 2.5).

“The king shall act as he pleases. He shall exalt himself and consider himself greater than any god, and shall speak horrendous things against the God of gods. He shall prosper until the period of wrath is completed, for what is determined shall be done.
He shall pay no respect to the gods of his ancestors, or to the one beloved by women; he shall pay no respect to any other god, for he shall consider himself greater than all.
37 : , perhaps Tammuz/Adonis (Ezek 8.14), or the Greek god Dionysus.
He shall honor the god of fortresses instead of these; a god whom his ancestors did not know he shall honor with gold and silver, with precious stones and costly gifts.
38 : , Zeus Olympios (2 Macc 6.2).

He shall deal with the strongest fortresses by the help of a foreign god. Those who acknowledge him he shall make more wealthy, and shall appoint them as rulers over many, and shall distribute the land for a price.
11.29–39: Antiochus's attacks on the Jews.
36–39 : Antiochus IV blasphemes the God of Israel and other gods.
39 : , the Hebrew text of this verse is garbled and the meaning is unclear but it seems to refer to the Acra in Jerusalem, which was occupied by foreign troops.

“At the time of the end the king of the south shall attack him. But the king of the north shall rush upon him like a whirlwind, with chariots and horsemen, and with many ships. He shall advance against countries and pass through like a flood.
He shall come into the beautiful land, and tens of thousands shall fall victim, but Edom and Moab and the main part of the Ammonites shall escape from his power.
41 : , ancient enemies of Israel (see 2 Sam 11–12; 2 Kings 3; Lam 4.21–22).
He shall stretch out his hand against the countries, and the land of Egypt shall not escape.
He shall become ruler of the treasures of gold and of silver, and all the riches of Egypt; and the Libyans and the Ethiopians shall follow in his train.
But reports from the east and the north shall alarm him, and he shall go out with great fury to bring ruin and complete destruction to many.
He shall pitch his palatial tents between the sea and the beautiful holy mountain. Yet he shall come to his end, with no one to help him.

11.2–12.4: The fourth apocalypse. History is framed in terms of military dominance that alternates between a figurative king of the north (kings of the Seleucid dynasty originating from Syria) and a king of the south (kings of the Ptolemaic dynasty in Egypt). Some details cannot be reconciled with nonbiblical sources.
11.40–45: The end-time. These verses anticipate a future eschatological battle using elements from Ezek 38.8–13; Zech 14.2; Pss 2; 48.
45 : , Antiochus IV died in Persia in x164 .
